_work.title: The Reception of Ancient Magic in Later Art
_work.creator: various
_work.place: Italy, England, France, Spain, the Low Countries
_work.rights: Public domain / CC BY (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
_work.blurb: How Renaissance, Baroque and 19th-century artists re-imagined the magicians, witches and prophets of classical and biblical antiquity — Circe and Medea, the Witch of Endor raising Samuel, the sibyls who foretold Christ, Lucan's necromancer Erichtho, Saturn the devouring melancholic, and the Isis-initiation novel of Apuleius. The works are dated to the later artwork, not the lost ancient original.

entries.title: Circe Offering the Cup to Ulysses
entries.work: The Reception of Ancient Magic in Later Art
entries.creator: John William Waterhouse
entries.rights: Public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
Waterhouse's monumental Circe, the archetypal Pre-Raphaelite image of the antique enchantress, painted in 1891.

## Iconography
**Circe** sits enthroned and frontal, raising in one hand the drugged **cup** and in the other the **wand** with which she turns men to beasts. At her feet a transformed companion lies as a **swine**; behind her a **mirror** reflects the tiny approaching figure of **Ulysses**. Lions and a pool of spilled enchantment complete the composition.

## Significance
The scene is from *Odyssey* X, where the goddess-witch of Aiaia drugs Odysseus's crew. Waterhouse makes her the calm, commanding [[motif:circe-the-enchantress|sorceress]] who became the Victorian type of the dangerous magical woman, descended from Homer through Ovid. The mirror device — showing the hero she cannot yet see — is the painter's own theatrical addition.

## For artists & game designers
A seated boss-enchantress, cup-and-wand in hand, with a victim already transformed at her feet: a complete 'beast-maker' encounter in one frame.

entries.title: Circe Invidiosa
entries.work: The Reception of Ancient Magic in Later Art
entries.creator: John William Waterhouse
entries.rights: Public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
Waterhouse's 1892 *Circe Invidiosa* ('jealous Circe'), the enchantress poisoning the sea-pool of her rival Scylla.

## Iconography
**Circe** stands tall in an iridescent green-blue gown, pouring a stream of **poison** from a shallow bowl into the water below, where the half-glimpsed form of **Scylla** begins her monstrous **metamorphosis**. A grotesque sea-creature rises beneath her feet; the whole canvas is bathed in venomous green.

## Significance
The subject is from Ovid's *Metamorphoses* XIV: spurned by Glaucus, who loves Scylla, Circe out of [[motif:circe-the-enchantress|jealousy]] poisons the nymph's bathing-place and transforms her into a monster. Waterhouse condenses the Ovidian tale into a single vertical icon of cold, deliberate sorcery — magic as envy made visible.

## For artists & game designers
A vertical poisoning ritual rendered almost monochrome green — a study in how a single colour can encode toxicity and malice.

entries.title: Circe (or Melissa)
entries.work: The Reception of Ancient Magic in Later Art
entries.creator: Dosso Dossi
entries.rights: CC BY 3.0 (photo: Sailko). Galleria Borghese, via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
Dosso Dossi's enigmatic enchantress in a wood, traditionally called Circe but perhaps the benevolent sorceress Melissa of Ariosto, c. 1520 (Galleria Borghese).

## Iconography
A richly dressed **sorceress** sits in a dark landscape holding an inscribed **tablet** of magical characters, an open **book** of spells beside her, surrounded by **beasts** and birds — men she has transformed, or whom she is releasing. Suits of armour hang in the trees.

## Significance
The figure hovers between two readings: the cruel Homeric/Ovidian [[motif:circe-the-enchantress|Circe]] who turns men to animals, and the kindly enchantress **Melissa** of Ariosto's *Orlando Furioso*, who reverses Alcina's spells. Dosso, court painter at Ferrara, paints magic as a learned, bookish art — the witch as scholar with grimoire and inscribed plaque — reflecting the Este court's taste for chivalric romance.

## For artists & game designers
The enchantress-as-scholar: a spellcaster defined by an open grimoire and an inscribed tablet rather than by wand and potion.

citations.text: P. Humfrey & M. Lucco, *Dosso Dossi: Court Painter in Renaissance Ferrara* (1998).

entries.title: The Wine of Circe
entries.work: The Reception of Ancient Magic in Later Art
entries.creator: Edward Burne-Jones
entries.rights: Public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
Burne-Jones's *The Wine of Circe*, the enchantress dosing the wine-cups as Odysseus's ships appear on the horizon.

## Iconography
**Circe** leans gracefully over two golden wine-jars, dropping her **potion** into the cups laid for guests. Two black **panthers** — themselves transformed men — crouch at her feet, and through the window the **ships** of the approaching Greeks ride on a green sea. The mood is one of poised, decorative menace.

## Significance
A key Aesthetic-movement image of [[motif:circe-the-enchantress|Circe]], the design moved D. G. Rossetti to write a sonnet. Burne-Jones reads the Homeric witch as a figure of beautiful, languid danger, the meal itself the trap; the panthers literalize the metamorphosis to come. It distils the Victorian fascination with the antique sorceress as *femme fatale*.

## For artists & game designers
The poisoned banquet as ambush — the threat is in the hospitality, with already-transformed victims standing guard as beasts.

citations.text: D. G. Rossetti, sonnet 'For The Wine of Circe by Edward Burne-Jones'.
F. MacCarthy, *The Last Pre-Raphaelite: Edward Burne-Jones* (2011).

entries.title: Medea
entries.work: The Reception of Ancient Magic in Later Art
entries.creator: Frederick Sandys
entries.rights: Public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
Frederick Sandys's *Medea*, the Colchian sorceress in the act of mixing a fatal enchantment, painted 1866–68.

## Iconography
**Medea** stands wild-eyed before her brazier, fingering a **coral necklace** at her throat as she prepares a **potion**; a **toad**, a shell, and ritual paraphernalia lie before her, and behind glints the **Golden Fleece** and a Japanese-screen frieze of Argonauts. Her clenched, trance-like intensity conveys the moment of incantation.

## Significance
The subject is the sorceress of Euripides and Ovid who helps **Jason** win the Fleece, then destroys his bride with a poisoned gift. Sandys, the most technically dazzling of the Pre-Raphaelite circle, paints [[motif:medea-the-sorceress|Medea]] as the supreme witch — barbarian, learned in drugs, consumed by passion. Famously rejected by the Royal Academy in 1868, the picture became a cause célèbre.

## For artists & game designers
The spell-mixing close-up: a single figure, a brazier, and a clutter of grim ingredients (toad, shell, coral) communicate active witchcraft without any fantastical effect.

citations.text: B. Elzea, *Frederick Sandys 1829–1904: A Catalogue Raisonné* (2001).

entries.title: Medea
entries.work: The Reception of Ancient Magic in Later Art
entries.creator: Anselm Feuerbach
entries.rights: Public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
Anselm Feuerbach's grand 1870 *Medea*, the sorceress as brooding tragic mother on the eve of her revenge.

## Iconography
**Medea** sits massive and still on the **seashore**, her two **children** asleep against her, while in the distance Jason's **ship** is readied. Rather than the act of witchcraft, Feuerbach paints the heavy silence before the catastrophe — the abandoned wife contemplating the murder that will be her sorcery's last and worst work.

## Significance
The German classicist Feuerbach treats [[motif:medea-the-sorceress|Medea]] in the noble, Raphaelesque idiom of his 'ideal' style, drawing on Euripides. Here the magic recedes and the human tragedy dominates: the enchantress is above all a betrayed woman, her supernatural powers the instrument of a wholly human grief and rage.

## For artists & game designers
A counter-model to the spell-casting witch: power held in reserve, all menace conveyed through stillness and the sleeping children who will become its victims.

citations.text: J. Ecker, *Anselm Feuerbach: Leben und Werk* (1991).

entries.title: Saul and the Witch of Endor (etching, Rijksmuseum)
entries.work: The Reception of Ancient Magic in Later Art
entries.creator: Anonymous (after a Baroque composition); Rijksmuseum print
entries.rights: Public domain (CC0). Rijksmuseum, via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A Baroque print of Saul and the Witch of Endor (Rijksmuseum), depicting the most famous necromancy of the Bible.

## Iconography
The **Witch of Endor** conjures within a dim, torchlit chamber as the spectral form of the prophet **Samuel** rises from the ground. **King Saul**, who has come in disguise, collapses in terror at the apparition's prophecy of his doom. The scene is staged with Baroque flourishes of smoke, gesture and dramatic light.

## Significance
The episode (1 Samuel 28) — the medium of En-dor raising Samuel's shade at the king's command — was the great biblical proof-text for [[motif:necromancy|necromancy]] and was endlessly debated by demonologists: was it the true Samuel, a demon, or fraud? Painters from van Oostsanen to Salvator Rosa and West returned to it as the supreme scene of forbidden conjuring sanctioned by Scripture itself.

## For artists & game designers
The canonical 'summon the dead' tableau: kneeling witch, rising ghost, and a powerful man brought low — staged in a single torchlit room.

citations.text: S. Schreuder, in Rijksmuseum online catalogue, RP-P-OB-63.922.

entries.title: Saul and the Witch of Endor
entries.work: The Reception of Ancient Magic in Later Art
entries.creator: Jacob Cornelisz van Oostsanen
entries.rights: Public domain. Rijksmuseum, via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
Jacob Cornelisz van Oostsanen's 1526 panel, which fuses the biblical Witch of Endor with a Northern-Renaissance witches' sabbath (Rijksmuseum).

## Iconography
In the foreground the kneeling old **witch** raises the shrouded prophet **Samuel** within a **magic circle** before the fainting **King Saul**. Around them swarms a full demonological landscape: **naked witches** brewing and **flying**, cavorting demons, and a smoking cauldron — the sabbath imagery of Oostsanen's own age grafted onto the Old Testament story.

## Significance
This is the earliest dated Netherlandish painting of the subject and a landmark in the iconography of [[motif:witchcraft|witchcraft]]: it reads the [[motif:witch-of-endor|Witch of Endor]] (1 Samuel 28) through the contemporary witch-craze, much as Hans Baldung Grien did in prints. The biblical necromancer becomes a fully fledged sabbath-witch, documenting how antiquity's magic was re-imagined in early-modern terms.

## For artists & game designers
A masterclass in 'period drift': a single canvas layering an ancient summoning with a whole contemporary witch-world of flight, cauldrons, and demons.

citations.text: D. Carasso-Kok & J. Levy-van Halm (eds.), *Jacob Cornelisz van Oostsanen* (2014).

entries.title: Saul and the Witch of Endor (etching, Rijksmuseum)
entries.work: The Reception of Ancient Magic in Later Art
entries.creator: Anonymous; Rijksmuseum print
entries.rights: Public domain (CC0). Rijksmuseum, via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A second Rijksmuseum print of Saul and the Witch of Endor, showing a different compositional treatment of the conjuring of Samuel.

## Iconography
The **Witch of Endor** stands or kneels at her conjuring as the prophet **Samuel** appears; the disguised **King Saul** and his companions recoil. The print emphasizes the moment of the **apparition** and the king's dismay at hearing his coming defeat foretold.

## Significance
The story of En-dor (1 Samuel 28) generated a long print tradition across the Low Countries and beyond, since it offered the rare spectacle of a sanctioned biblical [[motif:necromancy|necromancy]]. Such engravings carried the imagery of the [[motif:witch-of-endor|raising of Samuel]] into Bibles and emblem books, fixing the visual formula of witch, rising shade, and stricken king.

## For artists & game designers
Variant blocking of the same beat — useful for studying how the 'summoner / apparition / horrified witness' triangle can be re-staged.

entries.title: Erichtho (Sextus Pompeius consulting the Thessalian witch)
entries.work: The Reception of Ancient Magic in Later Art
entries.creator: John Hamilton Mortimer
entries.rights: CC BY 2.0. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
John Hamilton Mortimer's image of Erichtho, the Thessalian necromancer of Lucan's *Pharsalia*, the most extreme witch of Roman epic.

## Iconography
The haggard **Erichtho** works over a fresh **corpse** on the **battlefield**, reanimating a dead soldier so that he may prophesy the outcome of the civil war to **Sextus Pompeius**, who comes to consult her. Mortimer renders the scene with Romantic gloom — gaunt figures, ruined landscape, the horror of the reawakened dead.

## Significance
**Erichtho** (Lucan, *Pharsalia* VI) is classical literature's darkest sorceress: she robs graves, compels the gods, and forces a slain man back to life for a single, hideous prophecy. As a study of [[motif:necromancy|necromancy]] she stands behind Dante's and Goethe's witches. Mortimer, an English history-painter drawn to violent and macabre subjects, helped revive her for the Gothic imagination.

## For artists & game designers
The battlefield reanimator: a witch who raises the freshly dead to extract information — necromancy as forced interrogation of a corpse.

citations.text: J. Sunderland, *John Hamilton Mortimer: His Life and Works* (1988).

entries.title: The Cumaean Sibyl (engraving after Michelangelo's Sistine fresco)
entries.work: The Reception of Ancient Magic in Later Art
entries.creator: Giovanni Volpato, after Michelangelo
entries.date: engraving (later 18th c.), after the fresco of 1508–12
entries.rights: Public domain (CC0). The Met, via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
An engraving after Michelangelo's Cumaean Sibyl on the Sistine ceiling, the pagan prophetess who was believed to have foretold Christ.

## Iconography
The aged, powerfully muscular **Cumaean Sibyl** pores over a great **open book** of prophecy, attended by two **putti**. Her formidable physical bulk — Michelangelo's famous *terribilità* — conveys the weight of the oracular knowledge she carries.

## Significance
The **sibyls** were pagan prophetesses whom Christian tradition, following Augustine and the *Sibylline Oracles*, credited with foreseeing the coming of Christ; Michelangelo therefore set five of them, the [[motif:cumaean-sibyl|Cumaean]] among them, beside the Hebrew prophets on the Sistine ceiling. The Cumaean Sibyl is the one who, in Virgil's *Aeneid* VI, guides Aeneas to the underworld. Engravings such as Volpato's carried Michelangelo's design across Europe.

## For artists & game designers
The oracle as monumental scholar: prophecy embodied not in a frail seeress but in a colossal, book-wielding sage.

citations.text: Virgil, *Aeneid* VI; Augustine, *City of God* XVIII.

entries.title: The Delphic Sibyl (engraving after Michelangelo's Sistine fresco)
entries.work: The Reception of Ancient Magic in Later Art
entries.creator: Giorgio Ghisi (and others), after Michelangelo
entries.date: engraving (16th c.), after the fresco of 1508–12
entries.rights: Public domain (CC0). The Met, via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
An engraving after Michelangelo's Delphic Sibyl, the most celebrated of his Sistine prophetesses, associated with the oracle of Apollo at Delphi.

## Iconography
The youthful **Delphic Sibyl** turns sharply, unrolling a **scroll** of prophecy, her wide eyes and windblown drapery suggesting the seizure of inspiration. Of all Michelangelo's sibyls she is the most idealized and serene.

## Significance
Linked to the [[motif:cumaean-sibyl|sibylline]] tradition and to the great oracle of **Delphi**, she stands among the pagan seeresses Michelangelo enrolled as unwitting witnesses to Christian revelation. The placing of these classical prophetesses alongside the Hebrew prophets is the supreme Renaissance statement of the idea that pagan **divination** had glimpsed the truth. Engraved series spread her image across Europe.

## For artists & game designers
The inspired moment of prophecy — body twisting, scroll snapping open — captures oracular possession as a single dynamic pose.

entries.title: Cumaean Sibyl (after Domenichino)
entries.work: The Reception of Ancient Magic in Later Art
entries.creator: Angelica Kauffmann, after Domenichino
entries.rights: Public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
Angelica Kauffmann's copy after Domenichino's famous *Cumaean Sibyl*, the serene Baroque type of the antique prophetess.

## Iconography
The young **Sibyl** wears an exotic embroidered **turban** and gazes upward in inspiration, an **open book** and a sheet of **musical notation** before her — the Baroque convention linking prophecy with divine harmony. Her beauty and calm contrast sharply with Michelangelo's brawny seeress.

## Significance
Domenichino's original (Galleria Borghese) became the most copied image of a [[motif:cumaean-sibyl|sibyl]] in European art, and the young Kauffmann's faithful copy testifies to its fame and to how prophetesses were studied as models of ideal female beauty. The book and music encode the belief that the [[motif:cumaean-sibyl|sibyls]] sang the future in inspired verse.

## For artists & game designers
The gentle, turbaned oracle — prophecy as beautiful reverie rather than violent possession; a softer template for a seeress NPC.

entries.title: Sibilla Cumana (the original, Galleria Borghese)
entries.work: The Reception of Ancient Magic in Later Art
entries.creator: Domenichino (Domenico Zampieri)
entries.rights: CC BY 3.0 (photo: Sailko). Galleria Borghese, via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
Domenichino's own *Sibilla Cumana* of 1616–17 in the Galleria Borghese, the prototype behind countless later copies.

## Iconography
The **Cumaean Sibyl** sits in a richly coloured **turban**, holding a sheet of **music** and turning her eyes heavenward, with the neck of a **viol** and a **book** beside her. Domenichino's classicizing Baroque gives her a poised, luminous gravity.

## Significance
This is the source image whose fame made the turbaned, music-bearing [[motif:cumaean-sibyl|Cumaean Sibyl]] a fixture of European galleries — copied by Kauffmann among many others. The pairing of prophetic book and musical score reflects the long association of sibylline [[motif:divination|divination]] with inspired song, from Virgil's *Aeneid* VI to the Renaissance idea of cosmic harmony.

## For artists & game designers
The canonical Baroque oracle: study it next to its copies to see how one design propagates and mutates through reproduction.

entries.title: The Cumaean Sibyl (from the Famous Persons cycle, Villa Carducci)
entries.work: The Reception of Ancient Magic in Later Art
entries.creator: Andrea del Castagno
entries.rights: Public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
Andrea del Castagno's Cumaean Sibyl from his fresco cycle of Famous Persons (*uomini e donne famosi*) at the Villa Carducci, c. 1450.

## Iconography
The **Sibyl** strides forward in a feigned niche, holding an open **scroll** and gesturing, posed like a living statue among the cycle's heroes and heroines. Castagno's sharp, sculptural early-Renaissance line gives her a vivid, almost monumental presence.

## Significance
The **Cumaean Sibyl** was admitted to a gallery of exemplary figures because, like the Hebrew prophets, she was held to have foretold Christ — the same logic that placed sibyls on church walls and ceilings across Italy. Castagno's cycle is an early Florentine instance of the humanist *uomini famosi* tradition, into which the pagan [[motif:cumaean-sibyl|prophetess]] is naturally absorbed.

## For artists & game designers
The prophetess as one of a hall of heroes — showing how an oracle can be presented as a celebrated historical personage rather than an uncanny outsider.

entries.title: Saturn Devouring His Son
entries.work: The Reception of Ancient Magic in Later Art
entries.creator: Francisco de Goya
entries.rights: Public domain. Museo del Prado, via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
Goya's nightmarish *Saturn Devouring His Son*, one of the Black Paintings from the Quinta del Sordo, c. 1819–23 (Prado).

## Iconography
The wild-eyed god **Saturn** (Greek Kronos) crouches in darkness, clutching the bloodied, half-eaten body of one of his **children** and cramming it into his mouth. The image is reduced to its raw horror: a black void, a crazed giant, and the act of **devouring** itself.

## Significance
The myth — Kronos eating his offspring to forestall the prophecy that one would overthrow him — was read in the astrological tradition through the planet **Saturn**, lord of [[motif:melancholy|melancholy]], time, and devouring age (see Klibansky, Panofsky & Saxl, *Saturn and Melancholy*). Goya strips away Renaissance allegory to expose the [[motif:saturn-devouring|astral god]] as pure, lightless dread, an emblem of Time consuming all it creates.

## For artists & game designers
The purest 'devouring titan' image in Western art — proof that horror intensifies as ornament is removed: one figure, one act, total darkness.

citations.text: R. Klibansky, E. Panofsky & F. Saxl, *Saturn and Melancholy* (1964).

entries.title: Saturn Devouring His Son
entries.work: The Reception of Ancient Magic in Later Art
entries.creator: Peter Paul Rubens
entries.rights: Public domain. Museo del Prado, via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
Rubens's *Saturn Devouring His Son* (Prado), the learned Baroque treatment of the myth, painted c. 1636–38.

## Iconography
The aged, bearded **Saturn**, leaning on his **scythe**, bites into the breast of a writhing infant **son**. Above gleams a band of **three stars** — Rubens's reference to the contemporary telescopic 'triple' appearance of the planet Saturn (its rings, not yet understood). The old man's wings and sickle identify him equally as Time.

## Significance
Rubens fuses three identities long entwined in the tradition: the Titan **Kronos** who eats his children, the planet **Saturn** of the [[motif:astrology|astrologers]], and **Time** (Chronos) the all-devourer. The starry detail shows the painter's awareness of Galilean astronomy, tying the [[motif:saturn-devouring|myth]] directly to the heavens. The work stands behind Goya's later, bleaker version.

## For artists & game designers
A design where a single celestial detail (the three stars) anchors a mythological scene to a real planet — myth and cosmology in one frame.

citations.text: R. Klibansky, E. Panofsky & F. Saxl, *Saturn and Melancholy* (1964).

entries.title: Pythagoras (detail from The School of Athens)
entries.work: The Reception of Ancient Magic in Later Art
entries.creator: Raphael
entries.rights: Public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
Raphael's Pythagoras from the *School of Athens* (Stanza della Segnatura), the sage of sacred number absorbed in writing while a tablet displays his harmonic ratios.

## Iconography
**Pythagoras** sits writing in a great book, his brow furrowed, while a disciple holds before him a **tablet** showing the *epogdoon* and the **tetractys** — the diagram of musical ratios (the octave, fifth and fourth) and the sacred triangle of the first four numbers whose sum is ten. Onlookers crane to copy his wisdom.

## Significance
Pythagoras was revered in the Renaissance as the founder of the doctrine that number underlies the cosmos — the **harmony of the spheres** and the mystical [[motif:sacred-number|tetractys]]. Through Ficino and the Neoplatonists his arithmology fed directly into Hermetic and [[motif:kabbalah|Kabbalistic]] number-mysticism. Raphael places him among the ancestors of philosophy in the most famous of all images of the antique sages.

## For artists & game designers
The scholar-mystic defined by a single prop: the harmonic tablet that turns a writing figure into the patriarch of sacred mathematics.

citations.text: S. K. Heninger, *Touches of Sweet Harmony: Pythagorean Cosmology and Renaissance Poetics* (1974).
I. Pagliara, in *Raffaello nell'appartamento di Giulio II e Leone X* (1993).

entries.title: Moses and the Brazen Serpent
entries.work: The Reception of Ancient Magic in Later Art
entries.creator: Giuseppe Nuvolone
entries.rights: Public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
Giuseppe Nuvolone's Baroque *Moses and the Brazen Serpent*, the Old Testament scene of a healing image set up against a plague of snakes.

## Iconography
**Moses** raises the **bronze serpent** coiled upon a **pole** while the Israelites, attacked by fiery **serpents**, writhe in agony below; those who look up at the metal snake are healed. The composition swirls with tangled bodies, struck and saved, around the upright standard of the cure.

## Significance
In Numbers 21 God commands Moses to make a [[motif:brazen-serpent|bronze serpent]] (*Nehushtan*) so that the bitten may look on it and live — an image at the very border of sanctioned wonder and forbidden idol, later destroyed by Hezekiah. Christian typology read it as a prefiguration of the Crucifixion (John 3:14). The motif fascinated artists as a rare biblical instance of an apotropaic, quasi-magical healing object.

## For artists & game designers
The healing totem amid catastrophe: a raised standard that cures those who behold it — a built-in 'look to be saved' mechanic.

citations.text: M. Rossi & A. Rovetta, *I Nuvoloni: pittori a Milano nel Seicento* (exhibition literature).

entries.title: Psyche Entering Cupid's Garden
entries.work: The Reception of Ancient Magic in Later Art
entries.creator: John William Waterhouse
entries.rights: Public domain. Harris Museum, via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
Waterhouse's *Psyche Entering Cupid's Garden* (1903), illustrating the tale of Cupid and Psyche embedded in Apuleius's *Golden Ass*.

## Iconography
**Psyche** pushes open the gate into the enchanted **garden** of the unseen god, hesitating on the **threshold** of the invisible palace where her mysterious bridegroom awaits. The lush, secret garden and her cautious step evoke the wonder and dread of the marvellous tale.

## Significance
The story of **Cupid and Psyche** is the central inset of Apuleius's *Metamorphoses* (the *Golden Ass*), the same Latin novel that ends with the hero Lucius's initiation into the mysteries of [[motif:isis|Isis]]. Already in antiquity the tale was read allegorically as the soul's (*psyche*'s) trials, suffering and final union with divine Love — a reading revived by the Platonists and beloved of Victorian painters. Waterhouse treats it as a parable of the questing soul.

## For artists & game designers
The threshold of the enchanted unseen palace — the archetypal 'step into the magic place' moment, all anticipation and concealed presence.

entries.title: The Tabula Cebetis (Table of Cebes) — allegorical print
entries.work: The Reception of Ancient Magic in Later Art
entries.creator: Anonymous; Rijksmuseum print
entries.rights: Public domain (CC0). Rijksmuseum, via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A detail from an engraved *Tabula Cebetis* (Table of Cebes), the allegorical map of human life derived from a Greek text ascribed to the Pythagorean Cebes.

## Iconography
The print lays out the **allegory of human life** as a series of **concentric enclosures**: souls enter the gate of life past **Fortune** and Deceit, pass through the precincts of false and **true education**, and ascend toward the citadel of Happiness. Each personified figure is labelled, making the whole a walkable moral diagram.

## Significance
The *Tabula Cebetis* (*pinax*), a dialogue of the 1st century CE attributed to a follower of [[motif:pythagoras|Pythagoras]], describes a votive panel in a temple of Kronos that maps the soul's pilgrimage. Rediscovered and printed throughout the Renaissance, it became a favourite subject for emblematic engravings uniting Platonic-Pythagorean philosophy with [[motif:hermetic|Hermetic]] and Stoic moral teaching.

## For artists & game designers
Literally a level-map of life: nested rings of trials and false paths leading to a final citadel — an allegory that is already a game board.

citations.text: *Tabula Cebetis* (Pseudo-Cebes), ed. J. T. Fitzgerald & L. M. White (1983).

entries.title: Morgan le Fay
entries.work: The Reception of Ancient Magic in Later Art
entries.creator: Frederick Sandys
entries.rights: Public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
Frederick Sandys's *Morgan le Fay* (1864), the Arthurian enchantress caught in the frenzy of casting a destructive spell.

## Iconography
**Morgan le Fay** twists in mid-incantation, throwing on the enchanted, flame-coloured **robe** she has woven on the **loom** behind her to destroy King Arthur. Talismanic objects, a leopard-skin, and runic decoration crowd the chamber; her contorted pose and glaring eyes convey the violence of the working **spell**.

## Significance
Though medieval and Arthurian rather than antique, Morgan belongs to the same 19th-century re-imagining of the powerful magical woman as Sandys's [[motif:medea-the-sorceress|Medea]] and Waterhouse's [[motif:circe-the-enchantress|Circe]] — the *enchantress* descended ultimately from Circe and Medea. Sandys renders her with the same jewelled, claustrophobic intensity, making her a Pre-Raphaelite icon of female sorcery.

## For artists & game designers
Spell-casting as physical performance: a witch shown mid-gesture donning her enchanted garment, the magic dramatized through the body rather than through effects.

citations.text: B. Elzea, *Frederick Sandys 1829–1904: A Catalogue Raisonné* (2001).
